name: Diego Hernandez

bio.text: I study why some people refuse a partisan label, and how that refusal translates into political behavior. My research brings the methods of political psychology to understand a group that most accounts of partisanship treat as a residual category: the American independent.

My dissertation separates two routes into independence. Some people arrive through disaffection, a slow withdrawal from parties they have come to distrust. Others arrive through something closer to epistemic autonomy, a deliberate choice to weigh candidates and issues without a partisan shortcut. The two groups look identical on a standard party identification scale. They do not think alike. I trace the difference with pooled national survey data and a pair of survey experiments.

researchLede: The dissertation argues that pure independence is not one thing. One pathway runs through disaffection, the other through deliberative autonomy, and the two carry different consequences for trust, engagement, and how people process political information.
dataTag: Pooled ANES 2016–2024 · N = 13,061 · two survey experiments
